Notice Of Motion And Motion For Mandatory Relief From Judgment And Order Vacating The Judgment Under Ccp 473b Or In The Alternative Discretionary Relief From Default Under Ccp 473b
Off Calendar Aug-17-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearance.
RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF JEFFREY DULLEA's Motion For Mandatory Relief From Judgment And Order Vacating The Judgment Under CCP 473b Or In The Alternative Discretionary Relief From Default Under CCP 473b as follows: Plaintiff Jeffrey Dullea's motion for mandatory relief from judgment pursuant to CCP 473, subd. (b), is denied and alternative motion for discretionary relief from default and order vacating the judgment pursuant to CCP 473, subd. (b), is granted. Mandatory relief is not warranted here. Relief from a default judgment or dismissal is mandatory under CCP 473, subd. (b), when a proper "attorney affidavit of fault" is filed. (CCP 473, subd. (b).) However, "there is no mandatory relief for negligence which consists of failing to request a trial de novo after there has been an arbitration hearing in which the plaintiff and her attorney actually participated." (Brown v. Williams (2000) 78 Cal.App.4th 182, 189.) Mr. Dullea and his attorney participated in the arbitration rendering mandatory relief improper. Mr. Dullea has shown that discretionary relief is proper. Mr. Dullea waived trial by jury by failing to pay the nonrefundable jury fee of $150 by June 8, 2016, the date of the initial case management conference. (See CCP 631, subd. (b), (c), and (f)(5).) However, CCP 631, subd. (g), provides the court discretion to allow a trial by jury despite a waiver. A trial court abuses its discretion as a matter of law when relief has been denied where there has been no prejudice to the party opposing jury trial or the court from an inadvertent waiver. (Johnson-Stovall v. Superior Court (1993) 17 Cal.App.4th 808, 811.) Although Mr. Dullea has not clearly shown why he failed to post jury fees by June 8, 2016 and continues to cite an outdated version of CCP 631, there has been no showing that Ms. Oliveira would be prejudiced should the court allow trial by jury. Absent any showing of prejudice, the court allows trial by jury per CCP 631, subd. (g), despite Mr. Dullea's waiver. Mr. Dullea's filing of a "Demand for Jury Trial" on May 8, 2017 suffices to request a trial de novo. Although not cited by either party, the court in Hanf v. Sunnyview Development, Inc. (1982) 128 Cal.App.3d 909, 916, adopted the substantial compliance test with respect to the procedure for obtaining a trial de novo set forth in CCP 1141.20 and the California Rules of Court. Hanf is on-point authority requiring that trial courts give effect to requests for trial de novo so long as they substantially comply with the procedure in CCP 1141.20 and what is now CRC 3.826(a). Similar to the notice in Hanf, Mr. Dullea's "Demand for Jury Trial" alerted both the trial court and the opposing party of the election to take the case to trial, was timely, and showed the intent take the case to trial. The prevailing party is to submit a proposed order that tracks the Court's ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.
